values at the time of acquisition and apply the same to purchased land if the
acquisition costs cannot be determined reliably.

We also reiterated our recommendation that the Municipal Mayor
require the:

a. MGSO to follow-up the request from the Registry of Deeds regarding
the owner’s copy of titles of the seven parcels of land; and

b. Municipal Assessor and MGSO to facilitate the titling of the 12
parcels of land in the name of the Municipality by making available
the Deed of Donation/Deed of Sale and other pertinent documents.

We reiterated our recommendation that the Municipal Mayor require the
Municipal Treasurer, Municipal Social Welfare and Development Officer and
Municipal Agriculturist to continue adopting efficient and aggressive collection
strategies to enforce collection of the loan receivable accounts from the
concerned debtors.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the concerned
Municipal officials to submit to her office a report on the status of the
Computerization Program-Phase I and Phase II, afterwards, make a written
communication to the service provider requiring the latter to complete the
system programs and fix the noted gaps. Otherwise, the total payments of
P7,008,891.00 will be disallowed in audit.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor create a Disposal
Committee and prioritize the immediate sale of unserviceable properties
through public auction or other modes of disposal to avoid further deterioration
of the same and earn at least the salvage value of these assets and also, unclog
the very limited office space/storage of the Municipality.

We reiterated our recommendation that the Municipal Mayor require
the Municipal Planning and Development Coordinator (MPDC) to expedite the
implementation of all projects programmed under the 20% DF so that intended
beneficiaries avail the maximum benefits that could be derived therefrom.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal
Accountant to:

a. refund the P86,620.28 as liquidated damages; and

b. ensure that liquidated damages in future transactions, if any, are duly
recognized and deducted before payments are made to suppliers on
the account of its procurement activities.

We reiterated our recommendation that the Municipal Mayor instruct
the officials concerned to identify/determine, evaluate and address the causes of
the incurrence of operating losses in the operation of the Municipal Public
Market and Municipal Slaughterhouse.

We further recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the
Municipal Accountant to effect the necessary adjustment of affected accounts of
GF and LMEEU for fair presentation of FS.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal
Treasurer to comply strictly with the above regulation by depositing all
collections promptly and intact.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the:

a. MHO to ensure that all requested drugs and medicines are included
in the lists provided by the PNDF, and if not, secure authority from
Head of the National Center for Pharmaceutical Access and
Management for the purchased items; otherwise, refund the total
amount of P83,687.66; and

b. Municipal Accountant to ensure that the requirements for the
purchase of drugs and medicines are complied with before disbursing
government funds.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal
Agriculturist to exert effort to find the corresponding Animal Dispersal Contract
for all animals dispersed and prepare a complete list.

We also reiterated our recommendation that the Municipal Mayor
require the Municipal Agriculturist to:

a. complete the conduct of the physical count/inventory of the animals
and prepare and render the report thereon complete with supporting
documents as proof of the status/condition of animals;

b. remind the beneficiaries of their obligations as stipulated in the
Animal Dispersal Contract;

c. conduct a complete investigation on the circumstances surrounding
the death of the animals and if negligence was committed, compel the
concerned recipients to refund the cost of animals;

d. identify the recipients of the transferred animal as next beneficiaries;
and

e. prove with evidence the allegation of the non-receipt of 22 swine by
beneficiaries.

The Municipal Treasurer must also be required to collect the cost of the
swine from its beneficiaries in accordance with the dispersal contract.

Furthermore, refrain from implementing similar projects if sustainability is not
assured.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the LDRRMO-
Designate to submit to the Audit Team the Monthly Report on the Sources and
Utilization of DRRMF on or before the 15th day after the end of each month
through the LDRRMC and the LDC in compliance with Item 5.1.5 of COA
Circular No. 2012-002.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the head of the
concerned implementing offices to:

a. submit the list of all on-going government PPAs of the immediately
preceding year and those that are to be implemented during the year
under review; and the monthly monitoring report; and

b. inform the Audit Team within ten days after the award of the project
or before the start of the program/activity that the appropriate
project signboards and/or public notices are already posted to
facilitate validation by the Audit Team.

We reiterated our recommendation that the Municipal Mayor provide
the necessary support in enjoining all barangays to establish their own MRF and
to strictly comply with all other prescribed procedures and guidelines under RA
No. 9003.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Treasurer to:
prepare and furnish the Municipal Accountant the complete list of taxpayers with delinquencies for years 2002 to 2016 as of December 31, 2016; and
prepare a statement of the reason or reasons for such non-collection or non-payment and submit to the Sanggunian concerned together with the list of taxpayers with delinquencies  with a request for assistance in the enforcement of the remedies for collection provided in RA No. 7160 in order for the Municipality to have funds for other projects that will benefit its constituents.
We also recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Accountant to prepare the necessary adjustments of RPT and SET Receivables once the list of delinquent taxpayers is received from the Municipal Treasurer to reflect the correct balance of these receivable accounts, and to maintain subsidiary ledger for each taxpayer to facilitate monitoring and preparation of necessary reports/schedules.
We further recommended that the Municipal Mayor communicate the deficiencies/gaps to the contractor/service provider of the Program to fix the system and in compliance with Section 2 of PD No. 1445.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor create an appraisal committee/team to properly value the donated land as to their fair values at the time of acquisition and apply the same to purchased land if the acquisition costs cannot be determined reliably.
We also recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the:
Municipal GSO to follow-up the request to the Registry of Deeds regarding the owner’s copy of titles to seven parcels of land.
Municipal Assessor and Municipal GSO to facilitate the titling in the name of the Municipality by making available the Deed of Donation/Deed of Sale and other pertinent documents of the 12 untitled land.
Ensure that valuation and proof of absolute ownership are present in the recording/adjustment of the land in the books of accounts.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Accountant to make the necessary accounting entries for the proper classification of the computerization program and presentation in the financial statements.
Furthermore, follow-up from the service provider their action on the gaps in the generation of masterlists of real property taxpayers in the Treasury System and automatic computation of discounts and penalties.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal GSO to coordinate with the Municipal Accountant in identifying the PPEs transferred from Other Assets account to their respective PPE accounts and thereafter conduct physical inventory. If unserviceable, prepare the Inventory and Inspection Report of Unserviceable Property and facilitate the proper disposal.

We recommend that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Planning and Development Coordinator to maximize the utilization of the 20% Development Fund through proper planning so that all its programmed development  projects could be implemented immediately and completed within the year to ensure that intended beneficiaries could avail of the maximum benefits that will be derived therefrom.

We reiterated our recommendation that the Municipal Mayor prioritize the immediate disposal of the unserviceable properties in accordance with Section 79 of PD No. 1445 to avoid further deterioration and earn at least the salvage value from the sale of these assets.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the officials concerned to identify/determine, evaluate and address the causes of the incurrence of the operating losses in the operation of the Municipal Public Market and Municipal Slaughterhouse.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Treasurer, Municipal Social Welfare and Development Officer and Municipal Agriculturist to continue adopting efficient and aggressive collection strategies to enforce collection of the loan receivable accounts from the concerned debtors. Furthermore, refrain from implementing similar projects if sustainability is not assured.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor execute the necessary lease agreement with the market stallholders to protect the interest of the Municipality. Also, the Municipal Treasurer and the OIC-Market Administrator should be instructed to exert effort in collecting market fees, including the delinquencies totaling P243,757.50. The OIC-Market Administrator should further be required to strictly enforce the provisions in the Lopez Market Code of 2007 to prevent further occurrence of delinquencies and in order to increase the municipal income that could be used for its priority programs and projects.
We recommended that the Local School Board Officials strictly observe the provisions of laws, rules and regulations mentioned above pertinent to the utilization of the SEF.
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal GSO to renew PAR every three years or whenever there is a change in custodianship, in compliance with the above-cited regulation.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor (a) provide the necessary support in enjoining all barangays to establish their own MRF and to implement waste segregation at source; (b) strictly enforce and intensify the information campaign on the no-littering policy among the residents; and (c) strictly comply with all other prescribed procedures and guidelines under RA No. 9003.

We reiterated our recommendation that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Assessor to submit the complete and updated RPT Assessment Roll to the Municipal Treasurer to enable him to submit the certified list of taxpayers showing the amount due and collectible to the Municipal Accountant for her to set up the reliable RPT/SET Receivables.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Assessor was able to submit to the Municipal Treasurer the complete and updated RPT Assessment Roll summarized per Barangay.
observations.reason: The masterlist of real property taxpayers is not yet submitted by the Municipal Treasurer to the Municipal Accountant because of gaps in the generation of the masterlist of taxpayers that need to be addressed by the service provider. Observation and recommendation are reiterated under Part II, Observation No. 1.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the GSO and Municipal Accountant to reconcile their PPE records.
observations.managementAction: The GSO and the Municipal Accountant reconciled the RPCPPE and PPE ledgers.
observations.reason: Discrepancy between the RPCPPE and PPE ledgers amounting to P25,000.00 was due to the recording of undelivered goods. Adjusting entries regarding the undelivered goods were drawn in January 2017. Items labeled as “Reclassifed as Other Assets (PPSAS)” in the RPCPPE totalling P2,895,683.74 were noted and discussed under Part II, Observation No. 4.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Accountant to reconcile the ledger with the record of the GSO and make the necessary adjustments, if any. Also, require the GSO and the Municipal Assessor to make the necessary actions in securing copies of TCT of the Municipal lots and titling the untitled parcels of land in the name of the Municipality to protect said property from possible unlawful claims.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Accountant was able to make the necessary entry to record the titled parcels of land under JEV No. 4308. The Municipal Assessor already made request to the Registry of Deeds for the copy of title of the titled lots.
observations.reason: The Municipal GSO and the Municipal Assessor were not able to secure the title of the untitled parcels of land due to incomplete documentation to prove the Municipality’s claim on the said asset. Furthermore, the unreliability of the recorded amount in the Land account was also noted. Observation and recommendation are reiterated under Part II, Observation No. 2.

observations.recommendation: We reiterated our recommendation that the Municipal Mayor require the General Services Officer and the Municipal Agriculturist to give priority in conducting physical count/inventory of the animals and to regularly monitor the project’s implementation to determine the success or failure of the program.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Agriculturist submitted a physical count and monitoring report of the animal dispersal project.
observations.reason: The report submitted was incomplete. Furthermore, the assertions that were stated in the report were not supported with proper documents. Observation and recommendation are reiterated under Part II, Observation No. 9.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor coordinate with the Sangguniang Bayan (SB) to pass a resolution to request the COA for write-off of the account balances, in accordance with the provision of COA Circular No. 97-01.

We also recommended to require the Municipal Accountant to gather the required supporting documents and submit these to the Auditor together with the SB Resolution for the writing-off of the account.
observations.managementAction: A copy of Sangguniang Bayan Resolution No. 2015-204 requesting the COA for write-off of the account balances in the Other Receivables account was submitted. Moreover, the Municipal Accountant submitted a certification regarding the unavailability of documents to support said receivables. The submitted documents is for evaluation of the Audit Team.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Treasurer to transfer the remaining funds maintained at PNB and UCPB to the LBP accounts as soon as possible, in compliance with the aforementioned regulations.
observations.managementAction: Bank accounts maintained in PNB were already closed on November 16, 2016. The remaining balance totaling P844.23 that were presented in the Notes to Financial Statements were already transferred to the LBP bank accounts in January 2017.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Accountant/BAC Chairman to submit to the Accountant the lacking documents for further evaluation.
observations.managementAction: All lacking documents were submitted to the Audit Team.

observations.recommendation: We reiterated our recommendation that the Municipal Mayor instruct the General Services Officer/Inventory and Appraisal Committee to submit the IIRUP to the Audit Team for evaluation to facilitate the disposal of fully-depreciated and unserviceable properties.
observations.managementAction: The Inventory Committee was able to draft the IIRUP.
observations.reason: The draft of the IIRUP was only presented during verification of Management’s actions. Said IIRUP was not yet submitted to the Audit Team for evaluation. Observation and recommendation are reiterated under Part II Observation No. 6.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the:

 Municipal Engineer to implement projects funded out of the 20% development fund for the benefit of its constituents.

BAC to fast track the bidding process and refrain from awarding the bidder with incomplete documents.
observations.managementAction: Sixteen projects were implemented in 2016 out of the 29.
observations.reason: Eleven of the unimplemented projects in 2014 and 2015 were not implemented while two were reverted and rebudgeted. Furthermore, only one out of 18 projects was implemented from the 2016 projects. Observation and recommendation are reiterated under Part II, Observation No. 5.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:

instruct the Municipal Assessor and Municipal Treasurer to closely coordinate and reconcile their records to come up with a complete and reliable database of RPU in order that the agency could identify all taxable RPUs.

request the service provider to fix the gaps of the system to increase the reliability of information.
observations.managementAction: The gaps that were mentioned were communicated to the service provider through an e-mail that was sent in July 2016 and most of these gaps were addressed.
observations.reason: Gaps in the generation of masterlist of real property taxpayers in the Treasury System and automatic computation of discounts and penalties were not addressed by the service provider. Observation and recommendation are reiterated under Part II, Observation No. 3.

observations.recommendation: We reiterated our recommendation that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Accountant to send another demand letters to the debtors. Also, require the Municipal Treasurer to exert efforts to enforce the collection of the loan amortization from beneficiaries of the livelihood program.
observations.managementAction: During 2016, demand letters dated January 4, 2016 and September 16, 2016 were sent to the debtors.
observations.reason: The Municipal Treasurer was only able to collect P33,000.00 from the debtors despite of their effort to collect the total remaining balance. Observation and recommendation are reiterated under Part II, Observation No. 8.

observations.recommendation: We recommended the Municipal Mayor to require the OIC-Market Administrator to draw lease agreement with the stallholders in order to protect the interest of the Municipality. Said contract should indicate the terms and conditions which both parties have agreed. Also instruct the Municipal Treasurer to enforce the collection of the amount of P307,897.00 from delinquent stallholders.
observations.managementAction: The OIC-Market Administrator was able to draft lease agreement with the stallholders, indicating its terms and conditions.
observations.reason: Of the P307,897.00 worth of delinquencies in market fees, only P98,497.50 were collected in 2016. During 2016, no lease agreement were executed due to lack of authorization of the Municipal Mayor by the Sangguniang Bayan to enter into and sign the lease agreement with the market stallholders. Additional delinquent stallholders with uncollected balances totaling ₱38,857.50 existed during calendar year 2016. Observation and recommendation are reiterated under Part II, Observation No. 10.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the General Services Officer to renew PAR every three years or whenever there is a change in custodianship, in compliance with the above-cited regulation.
observations.managementAction: PARs of property, plant, and equipment assigned to the employees separated from the Municipality were renewed due to change of custodianship.
observations.reason: Despite of the audit recommendation, the Municipal GSO still failed to renew the PAR of the property, plant, and equipment purchased before 2015. Observation and recommendation are reiterated under Part II, Observation No. 12.
